coredata.dc:title: Influence of nitrogen-boron-sulfur triple-doping on the fluorescence emission of carbon dots and their sensing detection of copper ions

coredata.dc:description: This study used a one-step hydrothermal approach to synthesize three doped carbon dots, Cdots-1 (N-doped), Cdots-2 (N, S co-doped), and Cdots-3 (N, S, B co-doped), using aspartic acid, cysteine, and boric acid as raw ingredients. We employed XRD, TEM, Uv-Vis, XPS, FTIR, and PL techniques to characterize the morphology, elemental composition, and optical properties of samples. We found that doping is an effective way to control the core structure and emission properties of carbon dots. Among the three carbon dots, Cdots-3 has the highest fluorescence intensity and emits strong blue fluorescence. In addition, we found that Cdots-3 is highly responsive to Cu2 + with a linear equation of y = 955.02–1.68976x, which makes it a fluorescent probe that can detect Cu2+ rapidly. The fluorescence lifetime of Cdots-3 was shortened from 3.76 ns to 3.33 ns after adding Cu2+. The analysis shows that the fluorescence quenching of Cdots-3 is mainly a dynamic quenching mechanism accompanied by a standard static quenching process. Moreover, to verify the detection accuracy of the experiment, we used the linear regression model, random forest regression model, support vector machine regression model, and decision tree regression model to predict the detection model, respectively. Among them, the random forest model has the best prediction effect. The coefficient of determination reaches 0.9996, and the mean square error is 0.0167. It is suggested that Cdots can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the reliability of Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Influence of nitrogen-boron-sulfur triple-doping on the fluorescence emission of carbon dots and their sensing detection of copper ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study used a one-step hydrothermal approach to synthesize three doped carbon dots, Cdots-1 (N-doped), Cdots-2 (N, S co-doped), and Cdots-3 (N, S, B co-doped), using aspartic acid, cysteine, and boric acid as raw ingredients. We employed XRD, TEM, Uv-Vis, XPS, FTIR, and PL techniques to characterize the morphology, elemental composition, and optical properties of samples. We found that doping is an effective way to control the core structure and emission properties of carbon dots. Among the three carbon dots, Cdots-3 has the highest fluorescence intensity and emits strong blue fluorescence. In addition, we found that Cdots-3 is highly responsive to Cu2 + with a linear equation of y = 955.02–1.68976x, which makes it a fluorescent probe that can detect Cu2+ rapidly. The fluorescence lifetime of Cdots-3 was shortened from 3.76 ns to 3.33 ns after adding Cu2+. The analysis shows that the fluorescence quenching of Cdots-3 is mainly a dynamic quenching mechanism accompanied by a standard static quenching process. Moreover, to verify the detection accuracy of the experiment, we used the linear regression model, random forest regression model, support vector machine regression model, and decision tree regression model to predict the detection model, respectively. Among them, the random forest model has the best prediction effect. The coefficient of determination reaches 0.9996, and the mean square error is 0.0167. It is suggested that Cdots can be used as fluorescent probes to detect the reliability of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ is a metal ion closely related to human life and is used in various industries. It is also a trace element indispensable for human activities. However, above a specific concentration beyond a certain threshold, it can significantly damage both the social environment and human health (Zhang et al., 2024). Therefore, the development of a simple, fast, and highly sensitive detection approach is crucial. At present, the methods for detecting Cu2+ (atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, spectrophotometry and electrochemical sensing) all have limitations: atomic absorption spectrometry is inefficient due to its high cost and limited atomization (Wang et al., 2023; Hui et al., 2024; Ferreira et al., 2018). Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry requires a significant investment in instrumentation (Chen et al., 2023; Mierzwa et al., 2008). Spectrophotometry accuracy is affected by matrix interferences (Dorehgiraee et al., 2018). Moreover, although electrochemical methods are sensitive, they are still limited by matrix effects, strict pretreatment protocols, and specialized instrumentation requirements (Meucci et al., 2020). Fluorescence spectrometry is simple to operate in practical applications, and the equipment is relatively cheap. In simpler terms, the concentration of Cu2+ influences the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent materials. By analyzing the fluorescence response, it is possible to preliminarily assess whether the Cu2+ concentration in a solution exceeds the permissible limit (Xu et al., 2021).

Futhermore, fluorescence spectroscopy has received much attention from researchers. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Cdots) have unique chemical and optical properties and have been widely studied and applied in bioimaging, detection of metal ions, photocatalysis, and biosensing (Tian et al., 2021). This is an innovative fluorescent nanomaterial with dimensions smaller than 10 nanometers. Cdots have a more comprehensive emission range and narrower half-peak width characteristics, and their fluorescence emission is affected by other factors such as excitation wavelength, particle size, etc. At the same time, they have longer lifetimes, higher stability, and lower toxicity and are gradually replacing traditional quantum dot materials in fluorescence detection applications (Deng et al., 2018). At present, carbon dots are produced through both top-down and bottom-up synthesis techniques. Top-down approaches involve laser oxidation and electrochemical cauterization, while bottom-up methods encompass microwave synthesis, pyrolysis, ultrasonic treatment, and hydrothermal processing (Kong et al., 2024). Hydrothermal processes are widely used because they are simple and do not require complex subsequent treatment processes. The hydrothermal method involves the thermochemical breakdown of raw materials in a water-based environment under elevated temperature and pressure conditions. This approach shows great potential for practical applications.

Unmodified Cdots usually have low fluorescence quantum yields and are monofunctional, making it difficult to adapt to various detection needs (Fu et al., 2024; Nguyen et al., 2022). It has been shown that by doping different heteroatoms, the surface defects can be effectively adjusted, the electronic energy levels can be regulated, the optical properties of Cdots can be altered, and their fluorescence characteristics can be changed to adapt to different practical needs (Kang and Kang, 2021). Carbon dots incorporated with heteroatoms demonstrate enhanced stability. The doping of Cdots with heteroatoms has attracted much academic attention in recent years. Heteroatom-doped carbon dots exhibit improved stability. However, when metals and transition metal atoms are used, their larger atomic radii than carbon can decrease doping efficiency and result in uneven distribution within the material (Li et al., 2019). Therefore, doping with non-metallic elements that do not differ significantly from the radius of the carbon atom can effectively increase this effectiveness and reduce this inhomogeneity. Huang et al (Huang et al., 2018a). developed nitrogen, boron, and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NBS-CD) through an optimized hydrothermal synthesis approach. This innovation aimed to create a straightforward and effective fluorescence-based analytical method for detecting Ag+ ions and L-cysteine (L-Cys) in intricate biological fluids and live cell environments. Huang et al (Huang et al., 2020). developed an innovative and highly sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting copper ions. Citric acid was used as the carbon source, and the poly (acylamino amine) dendrimer (2G-PAMAM) was used as the nitrogen source in synthesizing the carbon dots. Functionalized NH2-CDs enable the rapid, sensitive, and specific detection of Cu2+, with a detection limit as low as 5 nM and a dynamic linear range extending from 0.005 to 600 μM. Zhang et al (Zhang et al., 2021). successfully synthesized nitrogen-boron co-doped quantum dots (B1N2CQDs), which were effectively employed as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ in river water samples and visualizing intracellular Fe3+ in biological systems. Keerthana et al (Keerthana et al., 2022). introduced a groundbreaking fluorescent sensor to detect picric acid, a key pollutant in industrial wastewater. Their innovative approach involved synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), which were subsequently functionalized with boron to produce boron-nitrogen carbon quantum dots (B/N-CQDs). When exposed to UV light, the researchers found that B/N-CQDs show a bright green fluorescence, a high quantum yield, exceptional water solubility, and great photostability. Their findings highlighted that variations in doping elements significantly influence the fluorescence emission properties. To broaden sensing applications and better understand the intricate luminescence mechanisms of Cdots, it is essential to carefully regulate and optimize their functional groups, compositions, and structural properties.

Carbon dots are highly likely to have precursor characteristic functional groups, thus narrowing the choice of precursors. In this work, we systematically synthesized N-doped (Cdots-1), N/S co-doped (Cdots-2), and N/S/B tri-doped (Cdots-3) carbon dots by rationally combining L-aspartic acid, L-cysteine, and boric acid precursors. Unlike conventional double-doped systems, our study reveals that boron doping in the ternary doped configuration (Cdots-3) induces two key changes: (i) XPS analysis confirms the formation of the C-N-B bonding structure. (ii) The FTIR absorption enhancement at 3450 cm−1 suggests increased hydroxyl group density. These structural modifications synergistically improved the detection selectivity of Cu2+ while increasing fluorescence intensity. In particular, a machine learning validation framework incorporating four regression models represents the first application of multi-algorithmic validation in carbon dots sensing, where random forests achieve extremely high prediction accuracy. This work demonstrates that controlled triple heteroatom doping can strategically tailor the carbon core structure and surface coordination environments to overcome the sensitivity-selectivity trade-off common in single/double doped-probes. This research not only enhances the accuracy and reliability of ion detection technology but also promotes the integration of interdisciplinary research and holds broad application value.

All chemicals, including L-aspartic acid, L-cysteine, boric acid, and metal nitrates (copper, cobalt, magnesium, nickel, cadmium, mercury, and iron 1000 μg/mL stock solution), were purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemicals & Technology Co. Ltd. (China) and used without further purification. Structural characterization was performed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-F200), and particle size analysis was performed using ImageJ software. Crystallinity was examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance A25x). Fourier analyzed surface functional groups in the 4000–500 cm−1 transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet IS10). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) provided elemental composition data. Optical properties were evaluated using UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy (TU-1901) and fluorescence spectroscopy (F98). Steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer to test fluorescence lifetime (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000).

Three types of carbon dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method (Zhu et al., 2024): (i) nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Cdots-1) prepared with 1.3310 g of L-aspartic acid, (ii) nitrogen/sulfur co-doped carbon dots (Cdots-2) synthesized from 1.3310 g of aspartic acid and 1.2616 g of cysteine, and (iii) nitrogen/sulfur/boron triple-doped carbon dots (Cdots-3) obtained by adding 0.6183 g of boric acid to the Cdots-2 precursor system. Each mixture was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, magnetically stirred for 40 minutes, and then transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined reactor. Heat treat at 180°C for 10 hours, then centrifuge (10,000 rpm for 10 minutes) to remove large particles to obtain a clear supernatant (target product). Fig. 1 shows a brief synthesis roadmap of carbon dots.

1 mL of Cdots-3 solution was mixed with 1 mL of various metal nitrate solutions (Cu2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe3+) in a 1:1 vol ratio to assess the selectivity of metal ions. Fluorescence intensity measurements were recorded after 3 min of incubation at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. For quantitative analysis, Cu2+ was detected by adding incremental volumes (150–650 µL) of undiluted Cu(NO3)2 solution to 1 mL of Cdots-3, and fluorescence spectra were collected under the same instrumental parameters. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Four regression models (Linear Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Decision Tree) were implemented using Scikit-learn (v1.3.2) in Python 3.10. The dataset was partitioned into 80 % training and 20 % testing sets. Spectral preprocessing included Savitzky-Golay smoothing (3rd order polynomial, 15-point window) and Standard Normal Variate transformation. Model performance was evaluated through coefficient of determination (R2) and mean squared error (RMSE) metrics, with hyperparameters optimized via grid search cross-validation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdots-3 crystal properties. The findings are shown in Fig. 2. It can be apparent that Cdots-1 and Cdot-3 have two distinct characteristic peaks near 28° corresponding to the typical graphite (0 0 2) crystal plane similarly, which is shifted upward by about 2° or so compared with the typical graphite peak (26.5°), which is related to the reduction of the distance between the sp2 layers (Preethi et al., 2022). In addition to this, there are also two distinct characteristic peaks near 42°, corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphite (Huang et al., 2018b). Interestingly, Cdots-2 has only one crystallographic facet, the (1 0 0). This may be because the S atoms were endo-doped into the central carbon core structure, causing this structure of the (1 0 0) crystal plane to be destroyed. Following the mixed response to the inclusion of boric acid, Cdot-3 exhibits a (0 0 2) crystal plane, suggesting that incorporating heteroatoms can effectively modulate the core structure of the carbon dots.

The morphology, size distribution, and dispersion properties of these three Cdots were examined through HRTEM analysis, with the findings presented in Fig. 3. A randomly selected carbon dots was analyzed for lattice streak analysis, and from Fig. 3(a), (d), and (g), it can be seen that the lattice spacings of Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdot-3 are 0.211 nm, 0.20859 nm, and 0.20733 nm, respectively, which corresponds to the (1 0 0) lattice plane of graphite (Ding et al., 2024). Also, from Fig. 3(b), (e), and (h), monodisperse globules, which are the generated carbon dots, can be seen. The difference is that the dispersion and homogeneity increase from Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdots-3. That is, Cdots-3 has the best dispersion and homogeneity. This is associated with the surface characteristics of the carbon dots. In addition, the particle size distributions of Fig. 3(b), (e), and (h) were counted using ImageJ software, and the results are shown in Fig. 3(c), (f), and (i). Cdots-1 has a median of 2.556 nm, and the particle size ranges from 1.212 to 4.805 nm, according to the particle size distribution graph. The median particle size of Cdots-2 was 4.63 nm, with a range of 2.315–6.211 nm. The median particle size of Cdots-3 was 3.1565 nm, ranging from 1.591 nm to 5.273 nm. This aligns with Fig. 3(b), (e), and (h), and the distribution of particle sizes is in line with the Gaussian distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XPS analysis of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to examine the surface functional groups of Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdots-3. The results are shown in Fig. 4. The broad absorption band around 3450 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of -OH and -NH groups, exhibiting a prominent broad peak (Amjadi et al., 2017). Stretching vibrational peaks of C-H and N-H were observed at 2821 cm−1, 765 cm−1, and 584 cm−1(Slewa, 2024). The telescopic vibrational peak of C3-N was observed at 2025 cm−1(Cai et al., 2017). In addition, the telescopic vibrational peak of Cdots-1 was also observed to be stronger than those of Cdots-2 and Cdots-3, which may be due to the higher graphitic nitrogen content of Cdots-1, resulting in a stronger signal. In the IR mapping, we also observed a CO stretching vibration peak near 1630 cm−1 and an N-H peak at 1594 cm−1, where the N-H is a bending vibration (Dhongde et al., 2024). According to earlier research, the peaks at 1384 cm-1 and 1352 cm−1 represent the distinctive absorption of C-C/CC and C-N/B-O chemical bonds (Long et al., 2024; Ali et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2025; Ma a et al., 2020). The stretching vibration of the SO bond (Gao et al., 2023), which is not present in the spectra of Cdots-1, is linked to the peak at 1114 cm−1. Preliminary evidence that the S element has been doped into Cdots-2 and Cdots-3. The lack of a distinct absorption peak at 2082 cm−1 confirms the absence of the -SH group and verifies the completion of the primary L-cysteine reaction (Mansour et al., 2024). The characteristic peak of C-N-B is present near 1300 cm−1 (Li1 et al., 2021). Other characteristic peaks of functional groups could not be observed in the IR pattern, which will be further discussed in the following XPS analysis. In conclusion, it can be preliminarily concluded that Cdots-1 contains N elements, Cdots-2 contains N and S elements, and Cdots-3 contains N, S, and B elements.

Fig. 5(a) through Fig. 5(e)-(i) present the full-scan XPS energy spectra for Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdots-3. Additionally, they include the high-resolution spectra of C1s and N1s for Cdots-1, C1s, N1s, and S2p for Cdots-2, as well as C1s, N1s, S2p, and B1s for Cdots-3. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, and N1s of Cdots-1 are 60.80 %, 32.04 %, and 7.16 %, respectively. The atomic percentages of C1s, N1s, O1s, S2s, S2p for Cdots-2 were 60.54 %, 7.65 %, 26.26 %, 0.08 %, 5.53 % (Fig. 5(e)). The atomic percentages of C1s, N1s, O1s, S2s, S2p, B1s for Cdots-3 were 46.90 %, 6.77 %, 35.83 %, 0.95 %, 3.97 %, and 5.58 % (Fig. 5(i)). The high-resolution C1s spectra of Cdots-1 (Fig. 5(b)) can be back-convoluted into C-O (286.1 eV), O-CO (288.8 eV), and C-C/CC (284.9 eV) (Li et al., 2020). The C1s spectra of Cdots-2 and Cdot-3 similarly exhibit the characteristic signals of these three peaks. The characteristic peak of C-O (286.6 eV) of Cdot-3 has a weaker signal than of O-CO (288.9 eV) in the high-resolution spectra of the respective C1s. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of Cdot-1 was back-convoluted into two characteristic peaks of graphitic nitrogen (N-C3, 399.8 eV) and amino nitrogen (C-NH2, 401.5) (Fig. 5(c)) (Wang et al., 2020). The high-resolution N1s spectra of Cdots-2 and Cdots-3 are similarly back-convolved into these two peaks. The difference is that the high-resolution N1s spectra of Cdots-3 (Fig. 5(k)) show comparable signal intensities for graphitic nitrogen and amino nitrogen compared to the high-resolution N1s spectra of Cdots-1 and Cdots-2. The intensity of the graphitic nitrogen signal in Cdots-1 (Fig. 5(c)) and Cdots-2 (Fig. 5(g)) is lower compared to that of amino nitrogen. The high-resolution S2p spectra for Cdot-2 and Cdot-3 are displayed in Fig. 5(h), where the S2p spectrum of Cdot-2 reveals two distinct peaks corresponding to C-S-C (162.9 eV) and C-SOx (164 eV) (Riaz and Park, 2022). The S2p of Cdot-3 also shows two convolution peaks, C-S-C (163.2 eV) and C-SOx (164.4 eV). The difference is that in Cdot-3, the signal peak of C-SOx is much weaker than that of C-S-C. The high-resolution spectrum of the B1s of Cdots-3 (Fig. 5(d)) is back-convoluted into two signal peaks, C-N-B (191.8 eV) and B-O (192.3 eV) (Li1 et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2020). All these results proved that Cdots-1 contains N, Cdots-2 contains N, S, and Cdots-3 contains N, S, B, and -COOH, -OH, -NH2, -SO2H, -B(OH)2 groups. Also, all three carbon dots are well dispersed in polar solvents. The results were corroborated with those obtained from IR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectral analysis of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6(a) – (c) display the UV-Vis absorption spectra of Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdot-3. These spectra show that all three carbon sites display optical absorption bands in the 236–350 nm range, which are associated with the π→π* transition of the carbon core center eigenstates (C-C/CC). Furthermore, the absorption extends into the visible region (Li et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2021). The difference is that the tail of Cdot-3 extends to about 439 nm, while the tail of Cdots-1 extends to about 382 nm, and the tail of Cdots-2 extends to about 422 nm, which illustrates that the light absorption range of the three Cdots is somewhat altered as they are obtained. Cdots-2 and Cdot-3 may be doped with more hetero-elements than Cdots-1, resulting in structural changes in these two carbon dots and differences in the characteristic absorption peaks. Besides, no apparent doping effect was observed in the UV spectra, which were analyzed together with the XPS results, and it was speculated that it might be due to the insufficient doping degree, the doping energy level changes being masked by the π→π* leaps or the weak effect of doping on the absorption properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectral analysis of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdot-3 at the same gear ( Fig. 7(a)–(c)) (blue line). Not surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity is lowest when the carbon dots are synthesized using only aspartic acid. Under UV light at 365 nm, Cdots-1 displays blue-green fluorescence, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7(a). The fluorescence intensity of Cdots-2 synthesized from aspartate and cysteine was substantially enhanced. While the fluorescence intensity was substantially enhanced, Cdots-2 exhibited blue fluorescence (in Fig. 7(b)). The fluorescence intensity of Cdot-3 increased by approximately 1000 upon synthesis, attributed to the reaction with boric acid, resulting in a bright blue fluorescence (inset in Fig. 7(c)). Additionally, a shift in the fluorescence color of Cdot-3 is evident, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7(d). It is shown that the fluorescence color of Cdots changes to some extent with the doping of different heteroatoms, which also reflects that the emission characteristics of Cdots are affected by the doping elements and the chemical state of the surface. Additionally, Fig. 7(a) illustrates that the emission wavelength of Cdots-1 changes as the excitation wavelength ranges from 330 nm to 400 nm, indicating a clear dependence on the excitation wavelength (LeCroy et al., 2017). At this point, two distinct maximum emission peaks are observed at 440 and 470 nm, respectively. As observed in Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c), the excitation wavelengths for Cdots-2 and Cdot-3 remain independent of the excitation wavelength within the range of 350–400 nm (Zhang et al., 2016), with both displaying their maximum emission peaks at 460 nm. This further confirms that the influence on the emission properties is related to factors such as doping elements and surface chemical states and that the doping of different heteroatoms, as well as the degree of doping, can affect the aberration of Cdots to different degrees, resulting in different degrees of defective states (Kansay et al., 2024). In general, defect states promote higher radiative recombination probability in the presence of optical pumping, which improves the fluorescence intensity of Cdots (Ivády et al., 2019).

Eight sets of 1 mL (concentration of 4.29 mg/mL) of Cdots-3 solution were taken and Cdots-3, 1 mL of Cu(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, and Fe(NO3)3 were added to each of them at a volume ratio of 1:1, and findings are presented in Fig. 8(a) and (b). We found that Cdots-3 has the most substantial quenching effect on Cu2+, and Cdots-2 acts slightly stronger on Hg2+ than on Cu2+. In addition, we have estimated the optical band gap (Eg) values of Cdots-2 and Cdot-3 based on Eq. (1), (2), (3) and Tauc plots (Zhong et al., 2023).

Here, a represents the optical absorption coefficient, ℎν denotes the photon energy, E
g is the band gap, ℎ is Planck constant, and ν refers to the radiation frequency.

From Fig. 8(c) and (d) (blue line), it can be found that the UV absorptions of both Cdots changed after the addition of Cu2+ solution, which suggests that some kind of non-fluorescent substrate complexes may be generated in them, while the absorption peak of Cdots-3 slightly blueshifted when Cu2+ was added to the Cdots, which is related to quenching agent and quenching mechanism (Sun et al., 2023; Omer, 2018). The optical bandgap values for Cdots-2 and Cdot-3, measured at 3.939 eV and 3.991 eV, respectively, are shown in Fig. 8(e) and (f). These findings suggest that incorporating heteroatoms can effectively influence the band gap of the carbon dots (Sun et al., 2018). Adding Cu2+ increases the band gap values to 3.974 eV and 4.023 eV, respectively, i.e., the difference between the band gap of Cdots-3 and that of Cdots-3 +Cu2+ is more significant. The band gap of Cdots-3 is larger than that of Cdots-2, mainly because boron doping reduces the surface defect states and enhances the stability of the energy level distribution. The addition of Cu2+ reduces the interference of the surface states on the electronic structure by interacting with the surface functional groups, respectively, leading to an increase in the band gap. In addition, different doping systems (nitrogen/sulfur vs. nitrogen/sulfur/boron) have different sensitivities to Cu2+, leading to differences in the magnitude of the band gap changes. Furthermore, it was concluded that the high selectivity of Cdots-2 for Hg2+ was attributed to the fact that sulfur doping provided a large number of active sites with high affinity for Hg2+ and that the strong adsorption of sulfur groups of the surface functional groups on Hg2+ resulted in a significant variation of the bandgap, which enhanced the detection of Hg ions. The high selectivity of Cdots-3 for Cu2+ is attributed to the boron doping that enhances the surface binding capacity to the stearic acid Cu2+, and the synergistic action of boron hydroxyl and nitrogen groups results in stronger adsorption of Cu2+ and a more pronounced effect on the band gap. This difference in selectivity essentially stems from the ability of the doping element to modulate the surface chemical environment and electronic structure, as well as the chemical properties of the different metal ions (soft/hard acids).

After that, we took 1 mL of Cdots-3 solution and added 150 µL, 250 µL, 350 µL, 450 µL, 550 µL, and 650 µL of Cu(NO3)2 solution (undiluted) sequentially. Fluorescence change values were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer after the samples were allowed to stand for 3 minutes. An excitation wavelength of 390 nm was chosen as the optimal value to monitor the changes at each volume gradient. The results are displayed in Fig. 9(a). This could be attributed to the lower concentration of Cdots-3 compared to Cu2+. The high concentration of Cu2+ surrounds the Cdots-3 solution, hindering the absorption of excitation light and the reflection of emission light. Additionally, the fluorescence is almost fully quenched upon adding of 650 µL of Cu(NO3)2 solution. There is a good linear range between 150 µL-550 µL with a fitting coefficient R2 of 0.98642 and a linear equation of y = 955.02–1.68976x, which is given in Fig. 9(b). Subsequently, we calculated the detection limit of Cdots-3 for Cu2+ using the formula LOD= 3σ/S, which is 34 µM. This fluorescent probe opens up a wide range of applications for detecting high concentrations of Cu2+. On the other hand, in this experiment, the concentration of this fluorescent probe is 4.29 mg/mL. If the probe concentration is downgraded, it may have a lower detection limit and broaden the application scenarios. To evaluate the accuracy of Cu2+ detection in complex environments, we used the probe to explore a mixed solution with a molar ratio of about 3:1 for copper ions and interfering ions. The results are given in Fig. 9(c), from which it can be seen that the probe has good anti-interference ability. This suggests that the nonradiative relaxation process and photoluminescence quenching can constitute the sensing mechanism of Cdots-3 for Cu2+. In other words, Cdots-3 responds well to Cu2+ and has excellent potential as a fluorescent probe for Cu2+.

To assess the accuracy of the experiment detection, we employed four different prediction models: linear regression, random forest regression, support vector machine regression, and decision tree regression. Linear Regression (LR) (Santiago et al., 2018) is a statistical model that analyzes the linear relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables. It is a basic supervised learning algorithm that predicts continuous-valued outputs by fitting an optimal straight line (or hyperplane, in the multidimensional case). In general, linear regression can be solved by least squares to find its equation, which can be computed for a straight line
y = b x + a
. There is often more than one factor that affects y in general, and the following linear equation can usually be considered: where the β solution is obtained using the least squares method

.

Random Forest (RF) (Niankai et al., 2022) is a supervised learning technique that leverages ensemble learning, enhancing model performance by generating multiple decision trees and aggregating their predictions. In regression tasks, Random Forest combines the predictions from all individual decision trees by averaging them to produce the final result. This approach not only improves prediction accuracy but also reduces the risk of overfitting. A kind of generalized linear classifier called the Support Vector Machine (SVM) (Hong et al., 2021) is employed in supervised learning for binary classification tasks. It determines the decision boundary by finding a hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the two classes, based on the given training data. It works by finding a hyperplane that minimizes the distance of all data points to that plane. Its hyperplane is:
w
T
x + b = 0
. The parameters ω and b represent the hyperplane normal vector and its intercept, respectively. Decision Tree Regression (DTR) (Park et al., 2025) operates by constructing a tree-like model to analyze and predict data. In this structure, each internal node corresponds to a specific feature or attribute, while each leaf node denotes a predicted output value. Constructing a decision tree involves a recursive approach that splits the dataset based on the most suitable attributes or features. This process aims to ensure that the resulting subsets produce output values that closely approximate the actual values.

Fig. 10(a) shows the image after SG smoothing (Huang et al., 2018c) and Standard Normal Variation (SNV). SG smoothing smoothes the data by fitting local polynomials while retaining the key features in the data. The SNV variation not only removes impurities and noise from the spectral information but also reduces the differences between different samples to the same range for better comparison and analysis. Fig. 10(b) is a heat map of the inter-sample correlation coefficient matrix, where the colors reflect the degree of correlation between the samples, and the saturation of the colors indicates the strength of the correlation. The figure reveals that the most significant correlations are observed between 150 µL and 250 µL, 450 µL, and 250 µL, as well as 350 µL.

After processing the features, four regression models were developed for analysis: the linear regression model ( Fig. 11(a)), the random forest regression model (Fig. 11(b)), the support vector machine regression model (Fig. 11c), and the decision tree regression model (Fig. 11(d)). The dataset was split into training and testing sets in an 8:2 ratio, and the prediction outcomes for each model are displayed in Fig. 11. The random forest model demonstrates the highest predictive performance, achieving a coefficient of determination of 0.9996 and a mean squared error of 0.0167. On the other hand, the linear regression model shows the strongest linear relationship with the fluorescence values after applying the volume gradient of Cu2+, with a coefficient of determination of 0.9866 and a mean squared error of 0.5244. The results show a strong linear correlation between the predicted and actual values, suggesting that the model exhibits high prediction accuracy.

Building on the previous analysis and discussion, schematic diagrams illustrating the ideal electronic energy levels and electron transfer pathways for Cdots-3 and Cdots-3 +Cu2+ were created, with the results presented in Fig. 12. When exposed to optical pumping (UV light), electrons in the lower energy state (HOMO) absorb photons and transition to a higher energy level (LUMO). Through non-radiative vibrational relaxation, the excited electrons subsequently revert to their surface states (Xiong et al., 2023). When Cu2+ is not added, the electrons in the surface state will again transition to the low-energy state (HOMO), generating fluorescence in the process. After the addition of Cu2+, the electrons relax to the empty orbitals of Cu2+ through a non-radiative electron-leaping channel, preventing the electrons in the surface state from transitioning to the low-energy state (HOMO), which also prevents the emission of PL, and the fluorescence quenching phenomenon occurs.

Noteworthy is the fact that the band gap difference between Cu2+ and Cdots-3 + Cu2+ is more significant, and the low-energy state electrons will have to absorb more photons to jump to the high-energy state. Under the same conditions, the resistance to emitting fluorescence is more excellent. In addition, a vibrant set of marginalized groups can be seen in the possible structure of Cdots-3 deduced from IR and XPS analysis results (-B(OH)2, -COOH, -OH, -NH2, -SO2H). The lone pair of electrons of these groups can be filled into the empty orbitals of Cu2+, and the amino and hydroxyl groups have strong coordination with Cu2+(Djiazet et al., 2024). Furthermore, the N in the B-N-C bond can also coordinate with Cu2+, thus increasing the site of Cu2+ interaction. It is easy to see that the abundance of marginalized groups inevitably leads to the abundance of coupling interactions.

Among them, the co-doping of nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and boron (B) elements enhances fluorescence by synergistically regulating the electronic structure and surface chemistry of carbon dots. XPS analysis indicates that nitrogen exists in graphitic and amine nitrogen forms, promoting π→π* transitions through n-type doping. The C-S-C bond of S introduces surface defect states and inhibits non-radiative recombination. The C-N-B bond of B induces p-type doping. It synergistically increases the hydroxyl density with the B-O bond, forming a hydrogen bond network to passivate the surface suspension bond. Band gap analysis showed that the optical band gap increased from 3.939 eV of Cdots-2–3.991 eV of Cdots-3, which was due to the electron-acceptor characteristics of B and N/S co-reconstructing the electron cloud distribution of sp
2 carbon nuclei. This multi-element synergistic mechanism enhances the selective complexation ability with Cu2+ by precisely manipulating the spatial coordination of surface functional groups (-B(OH)2, -NH2, -SO2H).

The fluorescence lifetimes of Cdots-3 and Cdots-3 +Cu2+ were analyzed by using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer combined with the formula

Sasikumar et al., (2024). The binding results are shown in Fig. 13. The data showed that the fluorescence decay time constant of Cdots-3 was shortened from 3.76 ns to 3.33 ns after the addition of Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+ accelerated the fluorescence decay process. The significant decrease in the decay time (about 11 %) indicates a dynamic interaction between Cu2+ and carbon dots, which is often related to the mechanism of non-radiative energy transfer or electron transfer in the excited state (Xu et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2025; Huang et al., 2022). Specifically, Cu2+ may act as an electron acceptor to capture electrons in the excited state of carbon dots through the electron transfer pathway, resulting in the dissipation of excited state energy in a non-radiative form, thereby reducing the fluorescence intensity. This metal ion-induced fluorescence quenching phenomenon is consistent with the characteristics of the Stern-Volmer dynamic quenching model, and its mechanism is mainly due to collisional quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Agarwal et al., 2024). However, according to the UV absorption spectra, there is a difference in the absorbance curves of Cdots-3 and Cdots-3 +Cu2+ in the range of 200–300 nm (the absorbance of the blue curve is slightly lower than that of the red overall), and the characteristic UV absorption peak at 284 nm disappears. This change in absorbance implies a weak interaction between Cu2+ and the ground state of carbon dots (e.g., partial complex formation) (Wu et al., 2021). We speculate that this quenching mechanism is mainly dynamic quenching, accompanied by static quenching.

This study used aspartic acid, cysteine, and boric acid as the precursor materials, and it used a one-step hydrothermal synthesis of three different carbon dots (Cdots-1, Cdots-2, and Cdots-3). he addition of heteroatoms has a major impact on the central structure of the carbon dots, as shown by XRD and TEM investigations. Analysis of IR spectra and XPS energy spectra revealed that the variety of marginalized groups of the synthesized carbon dots increased with the increase of the characteristic functional groups of the precursors. Moreover, the PL emission intensity of Cdots-1 is extremely weak and has an excitation wavelength dependence between 330 nm and 400 nm. The PL emission intensity of Cdots-2 is substantially improved compared to Cdots-1. The PL emission intensity of Cdots-3 is again enhanced based on Cdots-2, and both Cdots-2 and Cdots-3 are excitation wavelength-independent from 350 nm to 400 nm, which indicates that the doping of the heteroatoms can effectively change the emission characteristics of carbon dots. Furthermore, this study showed that the PL spectral characteristics of Cdots-3 were sensitive to Cu2+ ion concentration in Cu2+ solutions with volume concentrations from 150 µL to 550 µL and had a good linear range in this interval with a fit coefficient R2 of 0.98642 and a linear equation of y = 955.02–1.68976x, which suggests that Cdots-3 has a great potential to be used as a fluorescent probe for rapid detection of Cu2+. Further theories and calculations show that the fluorescence quenching of Cdots-3 is the result of the interaction between dynamic quenching and static quenching.
